article_title: Please stop overcomplicating your notes — these lightweight apps do everything Obsidian does
core_thesis: Obsidian, while powerful, can be overwhelming due to its upfront configuration demands (vault location, folder structures, plugins) and complex ecosystem. For users who get stuck in setup mode, several lightweight, open-source note-taking apps offer similar core features (Markdown, local-first, cross-platform) with simpler interfaces, making them better suited for those who just want to write without overcomplicating the process.
detailed_summary: The article argues that Obsidian's flexibility often leads to analysis paralysis, where users spend more time configuring than actually taking notes. It presents four alternative apps that prioritize simplicity while retaining essential features like Markdown support, local storage, and cross-platform availability. QOwnNotes is the most feature-rich alternative, offering scripting, encryption, and Nextcloud sync. FocusWriter is a distraction-free word processor ideal for focused writing sessions. Simplenote is the most minimal, cloud-synced option with fast sync and version history. Joplin is the closest Obsidian alternative, with plugins for backlinking and graph views. The article concludes that not every note needs a 'second brain' and these tools solve basic note-taking needs without the overhead. A comment thread debates whether power users might prefer code editors like VS Code with Git, but others note the article targets non-technical users.

entities.name: Obsidian
entities.role: popular note-taking app criticized for complexity

entities.name: QOwnNotes
entities.role: lightweight open-source note-taking app with scripting and encryption

entities.name: Joplin
entities.role: closest Obsidian alternative with plugins and sync

key_data_points: Obsidian requires upfront decisions on vault location, folder structures, and plugins, which can overwhelm users and lead to setup paralysis (🟢 relevant to your productivity: if you find yourself overconfiguring, these alternatives may help you focus on actual note-taking).

QOwnNotes is built in C++, making it super lightweight, and offers Markdown editing, live preview, tagging, subfolders, full-text search, AES 256 encryption, distraction-free mode, a browser extension, scripting engine, and optional Nextcloud integration (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops: scripting engine and Nextcloud sync allow customization and self-hosted syncing).

FocusWriter hides its entire interface until the cursor reaches the screen edge, providing a truly distraction-free writing environment; it supports autosave, spellcheck, daily goals, timers, and customizable themes (🟢 relevant to your student needs: excellent for focused writing sessions and avoiding distractions).

Simplenote is cloud-synced (requires account), works offline, and offers a two-pane layout with no toolbar, folders, or formatting buttons; it supports Markdown (no live preview), tags, and version history (🟢 relevant to your productivity: minimal friction for quick notes, but lacks backlinking).

Joplin is the closest Obsidian alternative, with local-first storage, open standards, tags, notebooks, full-text search, web clipper, to-do lists, end-to-end encryption, sync options, and plugins for backlinking and graph views (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used to organize research notes with backlinking, similar to Obsidian).

A commenter suggests using VS Code or Zed with Git for version-controlled notes, but others note the article targets users who find even basic file organization daunting (🟢 relevant to your programming: if you're comfortable with code editors, you might prefer that workflow for technical notes).
conclusions: Obsidian's complexity is a barrier for users who want simple note-taking; lightweight alternatives like QOwnNotes, FocusWriter, Simplenote, and Joplin provide core features without the overhead, making them better for users who prioritize writing over configuration.
For users who need a knowledge base with backlinking, Joplin is the best alternative to Obsidian, as it supports plugins for graph views and backlinks while maintaining a simpler interface.

The article's comment section reveals a divide: technical users may prefer code editors with Git for notes, but the article's target audience (non-technical users) benefits from purpose-built apps that reduce friction.
personal_relevance_summary: As a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast, you might find Obsidian's flexibility useful for building a knowledge base, but if you ever feel overwhelmed by setup, these lightweight apps offer a faster path to actually writing notes. Joplin's backlinking and encryption features are particularly relevant for organizing Red Teaming research securely. FocusWriter can help you draft reports or study notes without distractions. The article also reminds you that not every note needs a complex system—sometimes a simple .txt file or a minimal app is enough, which aligns with your homelab/devops mindset of choosing the right tool for the job.
extra_advice: Consider using Joplin with self-hosted sync (e.g., Nextcloud) for a secure, Obsidian-like setup without the complexity. For quick, disposable notes, Simplenote's speed and cross-platform sync are unbeatable. If you ever need to version-control your notes, you can combine a plain-text editor (like VS Code) with Git—this gives you full control and integrates with your existing dev workflow.
